text: The laparoscopic view shows a large, thick-walled duplication cyst occupying the right hypochondrium. The camera port is positioned in the suprapubic area, providing a clear view of the cyst.
The surgeon places the instrument ports and uses a needle to aspirate the cystic structure, followed by initiating the de-roofing process to expose the cyst contents.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to carefully dissect and preserve the mesentery during the laparoscopic small bowel resection.
The surgeon carefully dissects the roof of the large, thick-walled cyst located in the right hypochondrium, using precise techniques to avoid damaging surrounding structures.
The surgeon completes a near total excision of the duplication cyst, with visible preservation of the surrounding mesentery.
The mesenteric vessels are visible at the bottom of the surgical field, surrounded by adjacent tissue structures.
The surgeon has completed the excision of most of the cyst, with the remaining tissue visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon enucleates a failed duplication cyst or remnant embedded in the mesentery, carefully removing it without damaging surrounding structures. Two cysts are visible, with the smaller one excised along with the roof of the cyst.

The surgeon observes a section of the bowel that appears devascularized, indicating a lack of blood supply. The decision is made to excise this portion of the bowel due to its compromised vascular status.
The surgeon proceeds with an end-to-end anastomosis of the ileum, visually aligning the bowel ends for connection.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to excise approximately six inches of the bowel, ensuring the bowel is not twisted during the procedure. The device helps limit bleeding from the vascular structures during the resection.

The surgeon observes the bowel tissue, noting areas that appear dusky and congested. Using a vessel sealing device, the surgeon excises the mesentery, minimizing bleeding. High-frequency current is applied to further manage bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon prepares to ligate the mesentery before cutting the bowel, a step typically performed in open surgery to ensure proper orientation and minimize bleeding.
The surgeon uses a ligature to secure the margin before cutting it to open the bowel lumen, revealing the interior of the bowel.
The surgeon opens up the distal lumen of the small bowel, revealing the interior of the bowel segment.
The surgeon places a hitching stitch on the medial end of the bowel using an outside-in, inside-out technique, tying off the stitch to provide support for the anastomosis.

The surgeon confirms the alignment of the bowel ends medially before proceeding with the anastomosis using a cornel stitch. The Maryland forceps used in the procedure appear different from standard Maryland forceps.
The surgeon uses a custom mid forceps with visible serrations on the outside to manipulate the tissue during the small bowel resection procedure.
The surgeon uses a dissecting Maryland forceps, characterized by its unique design, to manipulate and dissect tissue during the small bowel resection procedure.
The surgeon begins dissection of the posterior layer of the small bowel using a dissecting Maryland instrument, carefully separating the tissue.
The surgeon performs a continuous suture on the first layer of the bowel, preparing to apply interrupted sutures on the second layer.
The surgeon ensures that the cirrhosa is included in the suturing process, using custom-made instruments to handle the tissue layers during the small bowel resection.
The surgeon carefully resects the small bowel, aiming to minimize the removal of mucosal tissue while ensuring all layers, including the cirrhosa, are included in the resection.
The surgeon meticulously locks each stitch in the bowel anastomosis to prevent laxity, ensuring a secure and tight closure of the tissue layers.
The surgeon applies continuous interlocking sutures to the bowel, carefully taking minimal mucosa while maximizing cirrhosa engagement to maintain luminal integrity.
The surgeon completes the suturing of the posterior layer of the bowel anastomosis, ensuring the continuous interlocking sutures are in place.
The surgeon leaves the suture long after completing a stitch, allowing for easier manipulation or adjustment in subsequent steps.
The surgeon selects fresh suture material, preparing to continue the suturing process.
The surgeon begins suturing the anterior layer of the small bowel, preparing to secure the tissue with precise stitching.
The surgeon applies a separate suture material to the posterior layer from the medial end, ensuring the knot is placed externally to avoid constricting the bowel lumen.

The surgeon takes the anterior layer of the small bowel separately using a fresh suture material, proceeding to the lateral side. Two independent running suture lines are tied off anteriorly and posteriorly, ensuring no constricting effect on the bowel lumen.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the mucosa during suturing to minimize its bulk and prevent pouting. The mucosa is invaginated into the lumen, with the suture emerging from the serosa, aiming to reduce mucosal protrusion.
The surgeon applies a second layer of sutures on top of the previously placed suture line, ensuring reinforcement without constricting the lumen of the small bowel.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis, revealing multiple threads in the surgical field. A medial corneling stitch with a long end is visible, along with threads from the distal posterior layer and the anterior layer being run through.
The surgeon approaches the lateral edge of the small bowel anastomosis, preparing to tie a knot to secure the sutures.
Two surgical needles are visible inside the abdominal cavity, positioned for the continuation of the small bowel resection procedure.
The surgeon is working on the posterior and anterior suture lines, with visible threads from the anastomosis procedure in the surgical field.

The surgeon connects the two suture lines by tying a knot with the threads that have needles attached. After completing the first knot, the needle is cut off from one of the threads and removed from the surgical field.

The surgeon uses the left hand to wrap the suture around the right hand or needle holder, initiating the second layer of sutures. The stitches are zero muscular, and the surgeon ensures adequate bite while keeping the thread short.
The surgeon cuts off excess long threads to prevent clutter and completes the interrupted layer of the anastomosis, ensuring the mucosa is buried effectively.
In the laparoscopic view, the small bowel remains unexposed to the external environment, highlighting the minimally invasive nature of the procedure.
The completed anastomosis is visible, and the surgeon prepares to close the mesenteric gap.
The excised bowel segment is visible, with part of a cyst attached to it, while the remaining portion of the cyst is left in place.
The surgeon completes the closure of the mesentery defect, ensuring the mesenteric tissue is securely sutured.
The surgeon irrigates the surgical area thoroughly and places a drain in the right hypochondrium before concluding the procedure.
